data.title: Canada Budget Deficit Widens Sharply in January From a Year AgoUpdate
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canada's budget deficit in January widened significantly from a year ago, the country's Department of Finance said Friday.

The department's Fiscal Monitor publication said the budget deficit in January came in at 5.13 billion Canadian dollars, or the equivalent of US$3.59 billion. The January 2025 shortfall was more than double the C$2.06 billion deficit recorded in the same year-ago month.

With only two months of data remaining in the fiscal 2024-25 year, Canada's budget deficit is presently C$26.85 billion, or wider than the C$25.67 billion recorded in the same year-ago period.

Previously, the Canadian government anticipated a deficit for the 2024-25 fiscal year of C$48.3 billion, or 1.6% of GDP. Economists at Bank of Nova Scotia, however, say they anticipate a larger deficit, in the C$60 billion range--or similar to the previous fiscal year--due to the repeal of some tax measures and weak output.

The budget shortfall could exceed C$80 billion in FY25-26, Scotiabank adds, arguing that it incorporates economic damage from the U.S.-Canada trade conflict and government stimulus to help affected workers.

Total Canadian tax revenue rose by a tepid 0.9% in January, reflecting a sales-tax exemption the government introduced in December and that ended in February. Meanwhile, outlays on government programs and day-to-day administration climbed 12.5% in the month.

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com

data.title: CIM REAL ESTATE FINANCE TRUST, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
CIM Real Estate Finance Trust, Inc., a prominent player in the real estate finance sector, has released its 2024 10-K report, detailing the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's activities and future outlook, reflecting its commitment to maintaining its REIT status and delivering value to its stockholders.

Financial Highlights

Total Revenue: $483.9 million, reflecting a decrease of $84.959 million compared to the previous year, primarily due to a reduction in the Credit and Real Estate segments.

Net (loss) income: $(292.3) million, a significant decline from a net income of $28.1 million in the previous year, driven by increased expenses and a decrease in revenues.

Net (loss) income attributable to the Company: $(292.3) million, indicating a substantial decrease in profitability compared to the prior year.

Net loss per share: $(0.67), reflecting the overall net loss and reduced earnings per share compared to the previous year.

Credit Segment Revenues: $389.988 million, a decrease of $63.492 million due to the suspension of interest income on certain loans and a decrease in the investment portfolio size.

Real Estate Segment Revenues: $93.525 million, a decrease of $21.531 million, primarily due to the disposition of properties.

Credit Segment Expenses: $603.837 million, an increase of $191.496 million, largely due to a significant increase in the provision for credit losses.

Real Estate Segment Expenses: $122.779 million, an increase of $16.905 million, driven by higher impairment charges on properties.

Business Highlights

Business Segments: The company operates in two primary business segments: Credit and Real Estate. The Credit segment focuses on acquiring and originating floating rate first and second lien mortgage loans, as well as investing in real estate-related securities and corporate senior loans. The Real Estate segment involves acquiring and managing geographically diversified income-producing retail, industrial, and office properties leased to creditworthy tenants under long-term net leases.

Credit Portfolio: As of December 31, 2024, the credit portfolio consisted of 68 loans with a net book value of $3.4 billion, and investments in real estate-related securities and other assets valued at $345.8 million. The company also held a diversified portfolio through its subsidiary, CLR, which includes first mortgage loans and CMBS.

Real Estate Portfolio: The company owned 187 commercial real estate properties, comprising approximately 5.8 million rentable square feet across 36 states, with a 100% lease rate as of December 31, 2024. The portfolio includes retail, office, and industrial properties.

Geographical Performance: The real estate portfolio is geographically diversified across 36 states, with significant property holdings in Ohio, Illinois, California, Wisconsin, and Florida. The South and West regions hold the majority of the first mortgage loans by value.

Sales Units and Dispositions: During 2024, the company disposed of seven properties and 11 condominium units, generating total consideration of $128.0 million. The company also acquired two new properties for an aggregate purchase price of $44.1 million.

New Product Launches: The company invested in a CLO subordinated note and originated $77.1 million in first mortgage loans, indicating a focus on expanding its credit investment portfolio.

Operational Strategy: The company aims to generate competitive risk-adjusted returns through a diversified investment portfolio and active management of its assets. It plans to adapt its investment strategy to capitalize on evolving market conditions and opportunities.

Future Outlook: The company expects to pursue a listing of its common stock on a national securities exchange when deemed in the best interests of stockholders. It also anticipates continued management of its commercial real estate lending business through its subsidiary, CLR, and aims to maintain its REIT status for tax purposes.

Strategic Initiatives

Strategic Initiatives: The company is focused on maintaining its qualification as a REIT, which involves meeting specific organizational and operational requirements. It has also been actively managing its real estate portfolio by acquiring two properties for $44.1 million and disposing of seven properties for $90.6 million, as well as selling 11 condominium units for $37.4 million. The company is externally managed by CMFT Management and its Investment Advisor, both affiliates of CIM Group, which allows it to leverage CIM Group's expertise in real estate and infrastructure.

Capital Management: The company has been actively managing its capital through various activities. It decreased its total debt by $756.5 million, reducing its debt-to-total gross assets ratio to 62.6%. The company also redeemed 7.3 million shares under its share redemption program for $45.0 million at an average price of $6.15 per share. Additionally, it declared aggregate distributions of $0.45 per share for the year. The company has a share redemption program in place, which allows stockholders to sell their shares back to the company under certain conditions. During 2024, the company received redemption requests for approximately 151.9 million shares, of which it redeemed approximately 5.6 million shares for $33.9 million.

Future Outlook: The company expects to continue utilizing proceeds from operations, asset sales, and borrowings to finance future acquisitions, loan originations, and other corporate uses. It anticipates that the Federal Reserve's potential interest rate decreases in 2025 may impact its interest income on floating-rate investments. The company plans to pursue a listing of its common stock on a national securities exchange when deemed in the best interests of its stockholders. Additionally, the company aims to maintain its REIT status by distributing at least 90% of its taxable income to stockholders.

Challenges and Risks

Market Risks: The company operates in a highly competitive market for lending and investment opportunities, which may limit its ability to originate or acquire desirable loans and investments. Additionally, the company is exposed to risks related to real estate-related securities, including CMBS, which are subject to market price volatility and credit risk.

Operational Risks: The company is dependent on single-tenant leases for a substantial portion of its revenue. If it is unable to renew leases, lease vacant space, or re-lease space as leases expire, its financial condition could be adversely affected. Furthermore, the company may face challenges in integrating new assets and managing growth, which could impact its operational efficiency.

Regulatory Risks: The overturning of the Chevron doctrine by the U.S. Supreme Court could lead to challenges in various areas of law, including energy, environment, taxation, and labor, potentially impacting the company. Additionally, failure to maintain REIT status for U.S. federal income tax purposes would adversely affect operations and the ability to make distributions.

Emerging Risks: Cybersecurity risks and cyber incidents pose a significant threat to the company, as they could result in disrupted operations, financial data misstatements, and damage to tenant and stockholder relationships. The increased prevalence of remote work has heightened these risks.

Financial Risks: The company has incurred mortgage indebtedness and other borrowings, which may increase business risks and hinder its ability to make distributions. Additionally, the company may be unable to pay or maintain cash distributions or increase distributions over time due to various factors affecting cash flow from operations.

Management Strategies: Management has identified several strategies to address these challenges and risks, including enhancing cybersecurity measures to mitigate the risk of cyber incidents and protect sensitive data, actively managing the portfolio to ensure diversification and reduce reliance on single-tenant leases, monitoring market conditions and adjusting investment strategies to remain competitive in the lending and investment market, and ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements to maintain REIT status and avoid adverse tax implications.

Interest Rate Risks: The company is exposed to interest rate fluctuations, which may impact its financial performance. Rising interest rates could increase borrowing costs and affect the company's ability to service debt. Management is focused on monitoring interest rate trends and implementing strategies to mitigate potential adverse effects on financial results.

SEC Filing: CIM REAL ESTATE FINANCE TRUST, INC. [ CMRF ] - 10-K - Mar. 28, 2025

data.title: UPDATE 1 - U.S. again postpones sanctions against NIS - Vucic
data.description:
BELGRADE (Serbia), March 28 (SeeNews) - The U.S. Department of the Treasury has delayed enforcement of sanctions against Serbia's Russian-controlled sole refiner NIS [BEL:NIIS] by another 30 days, president Aleksandar Vucic said on Friday.

"We made it! We got another 30 days of NIS sanctions deferral," Vucic wrote in a post on Instagram.

In January, the U.S. imposed sanctions on NIS, which is majority-owned by Russian companies Gazprom Neft and Gazprom, as part of wider sanctions against the Russian energy sector. The restrictions were initially set to take effect on February 27 but were later delayed until March 28. In the meantime, NIS sought removal from the U.S. Treasury's sanctions list and a further delay to their enforcement.

On Thursday, Vucic told the Financial Times that Serbia has been in discussions with both the U.S. and Russia about a potential solution for NIS.

"We've been talking to the Americans, to the Russians, to everybody" about finding a solution. "We didn't see any changes in the American attitude," Vucic told the newspaper.

"The Russian attitude is that they want to keep it at any price," Vucic said, adding that if a deal would be reached on the transfer of ownership of NIS, the price wouldn't be an issue.

When the sanctions against NIS were first announced, Gazprom Neft held a 50% stake in NIS, while its parent Gazprom had a 6.15% stake. In the meantime, Gazprom Neft transferred a 5.15% stake in NIS to Gazprom, cutting its ownership stake to 44.85%. Gazprom was not targeted by the sanctions. Serbia's government is the second largest shareholder in NIS with 29.87%.

Serbia currently imports crude oil only via the Janaf pipeline through Croatia. Vucic told the Financial Times he was "not sure that Janaf will stop transferring the oil" on March 28.

Last month, energy minister Dubravka Djedovic Handanovic said that Serbia has sufficient oil reserves for an average of three months.

data.title: Dollar Slips Amid Growing Economic Concerns
data.description:
The dollar index fell about 0.4% on Friday, slipping below 104 after reaching three-week highs earlier in the week, as concerns over the US economic outlook intensified.

A new round of reciprocal tariffs, including a 25% levy on auto imports, is set to take effect on April 2nd, raising fears about the economic impact of the Trump administration’s trade policies amid signs of weakening growth.

Meanwhile, core PCE inflation accelerated in February, exceeding forecasts, while personal spending rose less than expected.

Additionally, the Michigan consumer sentiment index was revised lower, CB consumer confidence dropped to a four-year low, and core durable goods orders unexpectedly contracted.

For the week, the dollar was little changed but remains down roughly 3% for the month.

data.title: E& Unit Reaches Settlement Deal, Signs Strategic Partnership Agreement in Morocco
data.description:
Emirates Telecommunications Group (ADX:EAND), d/b/a e&, said Friday a subsidiary signed a settlement agreement and a new strategic partnership deal to expand in Morocco's telecommunications market.

The Court of Appeal of Casablanca agreed to lower the indemnity in its competition probe against e&'s 53%-owned Itissalat Al-Maghrib, or Maroc Telecom, to 4.38 billion Moroccan dirhams from 6.38 billion dirhams. The amount is payable to Wana Corp, or Inwi.

In addition, Maroc Telecom and Inwi also entered a strategic partnership to establish two joint ventures to focus on fiber-optic passive infrastructure deployment and rolling out new telecommunication towers.

data.title: Lean Hogs Higher After USDA Shows Lower InventoryMarket Talk
data.description: Lean hog futures on the CME are up 0.3% in morning trade, staying up for the day despite the negative momentum on the commodities space in general. That's because the Hogs and Pigs report released by the USDA Thursday showed slightly lower inventories than expected. "Friendly report versus expectations," says Ben Johnson of New Frontier Capital Markets. "Supply expectations for the summer have been lowered." The focus is now on April 2 'reciprocal' tariffs, which is injecting uncertainty into markets after the PCE report from the Commerce Department showed signs of resurgent stagflation. Live cattle is flat today. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Ghana central bank surprises with 100 basis points rate hike
data.description:
Ghana's central bank on Friday raised its main interest rate (GHCBIR=ECI) by 100 basis points to 28%, saying that inflation remains a concern.

A majority of economists polled by Reuters had expected the central bank to hold rates steady at its monetary policy meeting, citing high inflation and forex weakness.

"The committee decided to raise the monetary policy rate by 100 basis points to 28%," new central bank governor Johnson Asiama said during a news conference.

He added that the decision was a taken by a majority of the members of the bank's monetary policy committee.

data.title: French Constitutional Council ruling deals blow to Le Pen
data.description:
France's Constitutional Council ruled on Friday that local politicians can be barred from office immediately if convicted of a crime, leaving the door open for far-right leader Marine Le Pen to potentially be barred from the 2027 presidential race.

The council issued its ruling in a case that did not involve Le Pen, but its decision means she will face the prospect of being unable to run for president in 2027 if she is convicted in an embezzlement trial concluding on Monday.

Prosecutors in the embezzlement trial have asked for the National Rally (RN) leader to be barred from public office for five years. A so-called "provisional execution" ban would be effective immediately even if she appealed.

Le Pen, who leads opinion polls for the 2027 election, has denied wrongdoing and accuses prosecutors of seeking her "political death".

Robin Binsard, a lawyer who represents a disbarred politician, said the Constitutional Council's ruling on Friday was negative for Le Pen as "the judges retain the possibility of ordering ineligibility with provisional execution".

In Friday's case, the council dismissed a challenge by a disbarred councilman from the Mayotte archipelago who disputed the legality of a provisional execution ban imposed on him.

In its ruling, the council said the provisional execution of an ineligibility sentence did not infringe on freedom of expression rights, and was therefore legal.

Anticor, a French anti-corruption association, said such bans were "one of the most effective means of removing elected officials who have seriously undermined democracy from public life."

"This welcome decision comes amid growing distrust among certain political leaders toward the judicial authority, which could undermine the rule of law," it said.

In Le Pen's case, prosecutors have asked judges to impose an immediate five-year ban regardless of any appeal, via the same provisional execution measure.

Any provisional execution ban would not force Le Pen's removal from her seat in parliament until her mandate ends, but it would prevent her from running in any new electoral contest.

Le Pen, the RN and some two dozen party figures are accused of diverting over 3 million euros ($3.27 million) of European Parliament funds to pay staff working for the party in France.

The defendants say the money was used legitimately and that the allegations incorporate too narrow a definition of what a parliamentary assistant does.

data.title: UN chief: situation in South Sudan 'darkly reminiscent' of civil wars
data.description:
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said on Friday that a peace agreement in South Sudan was in a shambles, telling the country's leaders to put down the weapons and put all the people of South Sudan first.

"Let's not mince words: What we are seeing is darkly reminiscent of the 2013 and 2016 civil wars, which killed 400,000 people," Guterres told reporters.

First Vice President Riek Machar was put under house arrest in the capital Juba on Wednesday, in effect voiding a 2018 peace deal that ended a five-year civil war and brought Machar and President Salva Kiir into a fragile power-sharing government.

On Friday, the information minister accused Machar of seeking to launch a rebellion against the government.

"All the dark clouds of a perfect storm have descended upon the people of the world's newest country – and one of the poorest," said Guterres, listing a security emergency, political upheaval, "a humanitarian nightmare", a displacement and funding crisis and an economic meltdown.

"Meanwhile, ethnic and political targeting by security forces – coupled with the spread of misinformation on social media – is lighting the fuse for even worse," he added.

The U.N. Mission in South Sudan "is working around the clock to ease tensions – engaging all parties and boosting protection of civilians," Guterres said.

data.title: Metals Company to Apply for Deep Sea Mining Permits in International Waters Under US Mining Code
data.description:
The Metals Company is applying for mining exploration licenses and commercial recovery permits in international waters under existing US legislation rather than waiting for regulations under the International Seabed Authority, or ISA.

The company said Thursday it has started the application process through its US subsidiary with the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration under the US Department of Commerce.

The company said the US seabed mining code is the best way to secure a permit for mining deep-sea mineral resources "in a timely manner."

The Deep Seabed Hard Mineral Resources Act provides a "robust, predictable regulatory regime," in contrast with the ISA, which has not yet adopted the Exploitation Regulations "in breach of its treaty obligations under UNCLOS and the 1994 Agreement," the company said.

The company said it has met with officials in the White House and US Congress.

"We're encouraged by the growing recognition in Washington that nodules represent a strategic opportunity for America — and we're moving forward with urgency," said Metals Company Chairman and CEO Gerard Barron.

data.title: REG - Tatton Asset Mgt PLC - Director Appointment
data.description:
RNS Number : 7615C Tatton Asset Management PLC 28 March 2025  

28 March 2025

Tatton Asset Management plc

(or "the Company") 

Director Appointment

Tatton Asset Management PLC (AIM: TAM), the investment management and IFA support services group is pleased

to announce the appointment of Philippa (Pippa) Hamnett as Independent Non-Executive Director.

Pippa brings extensive capital markets experience as a former senior investment banking and corporate finance professional. Her career to date has spanned investment banking, corporate finance and corporate broking roles at Merrill Lynch, Collins Stewart, Canaccord Genuity, and Zeus Capital. Specialising in IPOs and secondary equity raises, with a focus on Financial Institutions, Pippa has worked closely with executive boards, private equity owners, and advisory partners to advise, execute and communicate on corporate development and continuing obligations in the public markets. Most recently, she was Chief of Staff at a venture capital backed engineering spin-out from the University of Oxford. Pippa has a Masters in Biochemistry from the University of Oxford.

Roger Cornick,  Chairman of Tatton Asset Management Plc: "I am delighted to welcome Pippa as a Non-Executive Director. Her appointment further strengthens the depth and diversity of expertise on our Board, and we look forward to her contributions."

The following information is provided in accordance with Rule 17 and Schedule Two (g) of the AIM Rules for Companies:

Pippa does not currently hold any Directorships or Partnerships and has not held any Directorship or Partnerships in the past five years.

As of the date of this announcement, Pippa does not hold ordinary shares in the capital of the Company.

There is no further information to be disclosed pursuant to Schedule Two (g) of the AIM Rules for Companies.

data.title: US bank regulators to rescind updated fair lending rules following bank litigation - statement
data.description:
U.S. bank regulators announced Friday they intended to rescind a 2023 update to fair lending rules for banks and reinstate the prior requirements, citing a legal challenge from the banking industry over the new framework.

In a joint statement, the Federal Reserve, Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation and Office of the Comptroller of the Currency said they intend to issue a proposal reinstating prior rules enforcing the Community Reinvestment Act, a 1977 law that requires banks to service local communities.
